6. **Rejected**: Exponential XP curve (Halo Infinite mistake), no catch-up (returning player churn), premium track that's just 'free + skin' (not enough value to convert), season-end deletion of player items (rage).

database_design.schema: Postgres seasons: id, code (e.g., 's5'), starts_at, ends_at, max_tier INT, xp_per_tier INT, premium_price_usd NUMERIC. battle_pass_rewards: season_id, tier INT, track ENUM(free, premium), reward_type, reward_payload JSONB. player_battle_pass: player_id, season_id, current_tier INT, current_xp INT, premium_active BOOLEAN, premium_purchased_at TIMESTAMPTZ, tiers_claimed JSONB (array of {tier, track}), last_xp_at. xp_grants_log: id, player_id, season_id, source TEXT, amount INT, multiplier NUMERIC, created_at, idempotency_key UNIQUE.
database_design.indexes: player_battle_pass: PK (player_id, season_id). xp_grants_log: (player_id, created_at DESC), UNIQUE (idempotency_key). battle_pass_rewards: (season_id, tier, track) PK.
database_design.denormalization: current_tier and current_xp denormalized on player_battle_pass (recomputable from xp_grants_log). Player's current battle pass cached in Redis for HUD reads.
database_design.transactions: XP grant + tier check + reward grant in single Postgres transaction (SELECT FOR UPDATE on player_battle_pass). Tier crossings emit notifications post-commit.

visualization_strategy: Horizontal scrolling tier track mimics a journey. Auto-scrolls to current tier on load — player immediately sees progress. Free track on top row, premium on bottom — clear visual hierarchy without ambiguity. Premium rewards locked behind subtle dark overlay with 'PREMIUM' label — sales-driving without aggression. Catch-up bonus shown as amber tag in header — urgency without panic. Tier cross celebration (full-screen modal): tier number bursts in, reward materializes, particle burst, fanfare — the 90 tier transitions are 90 dopamine moments. Color-blind: unlocked indicated by checkmark + green; locked indicated by reduced opacity. Reduced motion: tier reveal becomes a simple fade.
balance_considerations: Watch % of free-track players reaching tier 50 (target 70%), tier 100 (target 30% F2P, 75% premium). If F2P completion <50%, XP curve too slow — backlash incoming. Watch premium purchase rate (target 8-15% of MAU). Catch-up bonus engagement: % of returning-after-30-days who hit tier 30+ in remaining time. Final-week revenue spike (target 25-30% of season total — strong FOMO). A/B test premium price: $9.99 vs $14.99 — usually $9.99 wins on volume.
exploit_vectors_addressed: XP farming via private matches — XP grants only fire in ranked/competitive game modes; private matches get 0 XP.
Tier-skip purchase abuse — each tier-skip costs more than the last (anti-whale), capped at 50 skips per season.
Premium purchase via stolen credit card then refund — premium track frozen if associated purchase chargebacks within 7 days.
Battle pass XP duplication via match-end retry — idempotency key on (player_id, match_id, source) prevents double-credit.
Account-sharing to complete two battle passes — device fingerprint + IP analysis flags suspicious co-occurrence.
observability: Log xp_granted, tier_crossed, premium_purchased, reward_claimed. Metrics: XP earn rate p50/p95 (catch broken sources), tier distribution at season mid/end, premium conversion rate, F2P completion rate, late-season catch-up effectiveness. Designer dashboard: per-tier reward 'sticky' value (which tiers get the most return play within 24h after unlock).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior copies an exponential XP curve from another game — tier 50 needs 5x tier 49's XP. Players hit tier 70 and quit ('not worth grinding'). Then: no catch-up — players who join in week 8 see they can't realistically finish, don't bother, churn. Then: season end deletes premium currency — Reddit explosion, refund requests. Halo Infinite did almost exactly this; lead designer publicly apologized; battle pass economy rebuilt mid-season.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Fortnite (linear curve, achievable tiers, season-ending events as cultural moments), Apex Legends (level-up XP source clear). DONE BADLY: Halo Infinite launch — required ~25 hours per battle pass tier, daily challenges that gave 50 XP toward 1000XP/tier requirement, players felt cheated. 343 publicly apologized, restructured pass mid-season. Recovery took 2 years. Lesson: don't ship a battle pass without playtesting the entire 100-tier journey.

game_genre_context: Real-time turn-based strategy or card game (Hearthstone, Marvel Snap, Clash Royale clash) where two players' state must remain perfectly synchronized despite network jitter, disconnects, and timeouts. A single desync = corrupted match = refund tickets.
title: Authoritative Server Game State with Client Prediction, Chess Clocks, and Mid-Match Reconnect
design_intent: Build a turn-based multiplayer state machine where the server is authoritative for ALL game state (no client trust), with chess-clock timeouts per player, client-side prediction for responsiveness, desync detection via state hashing, and full state restoration when a player reconnects mid-match. Hearthstone does this brilliantly — drop your connection mid-match, come back 90 seconds later, full state restored, your turn still active.
mathematical_foundation: Chess clock: each player has time_remaining_ms decrementing in real-time during their turn. Server tracks last_turn_started_at and computes elapsed = now - last_turn_started_at. Time grant per move (Fischer increment): +5s per move played. Timeout = forfeit. State hash: SHA-256 over canonical JSON serialization of game state — both client and server compute, mismatch = desync. Network sync interval: server pushes state delta every 100ms during turn; full state every 5s as keyframe.
psychology_notes: Chess clocks add tactical pressure (Marvel Snap's 1-min turns drive purchases of 'Snap Pro' for tournament). Reconnect-friendly design earns trust: knowing 'my disconnect won't ruin my match' lowers anxiety about playing in unreliable networks. Client prediction (instant response to player input) FEELS responsive even on 200ms ping. Watching opponent's turn-clock tick down creates suspense ('they're agonizing over a move').
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Match begins, server initializes GameState in Redis keyed by match_id, both players' WebSockets joined to match room. Player action posted via socket → server validates against current state → applies if legal → broadcasts state delta to both players. Client predicts response immediately on action send (optimistic), reconciles when server confirms. Turn timeout fires server-side, automatically passes turn.

2. **Math**: Server is single source of truth. Action validation rejects illegal moves (e.g., playing a card not in hand) — client UI prevents most but server enforces. State hash on every action: server includes hash in delta; client recomputes own state and verifies match. Mismatch triggers full-state re-sync.

3. **Edge cases**: Player disconnects mid-turn — server pauses turn clock for 30s grace, then resumes counting down. If player reconnects, replay all state from match start (or last keyframe). If clock expires while disconnected, forfeit. Both players simultaneously submit actions in a quick-action game — server processes in receive order, second is rejected with state delta showing why. Server crashes — match state persisted in Redis with 1h TTL, replayed from action log on recovery.

4. **Visual**: Turn timer ALWAYS visible (radial sweep or bar). Opponent's turn shown with their clock counting down — creates suspense. Action card animations match server timing (don't show 'card played' until server confirms — prevents mispredict embarrassment). Reconnect: 'Reconnecting...' overlay with 30s timer; on success, brief state-restore animation showing what changed.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All game logic server-side. Client can't 'pay 0 mana for a card' — server checks resource balance. Random effects (card draws, dice rolls) use server-seeded PRNG, never client-rolled. Replay log allows post-match validation of every action.

6. **Rejected**: Peer-to-peer (cheaters host games), client-authoritative damage (cheaters inflate), no reconnect (mobile disconnects = lost match = uninstall), polling for state (websockets only).

database_design.schema: Redis HASH `match:{id}:state` with full game state JSON. Redis LIST `match:{id}:actions` append-only log of all actions. Postgres matches: id, player_a, player_b, started_at, ended_at, winner, action_log JSONB (post-match archive), state_hash_history JSONB[]. Player session: socket_id, current_match_id, last_seen_at.
database_design.indexes: Redis is the hot path. Postgres matches: (player_a, started_at), (player_b, started_at), (ended_at) for analytics.
database_design.denormalization: Match state denormalized into Redis hash for sub-ms reads. Action log dual-written (Redis live + Postgres archive at match end).
database_design.transactions: State updates use Redis WATCH/MULTI/EXEC for optimistic concurrency. Action processing is atomic: validate → apply → log → broadcast. Server crash mid-action is recoverable by replay.

visualization_strategy: Active player's HUD section gets amber ring + glow — instant 'who's turn' communication. Clock turns red and pulses at <10s remaining — pressure without panic. Cards lift on hover (anticipation), disabled cards desaturated (clear affordance). Opponent's hand shown card-back, count visible (info parity without revealing). Reconnect overlay is calm, not alarming ('we'll be right back', not 'CONNECTION LOST'). Optimistic prediction makes plays feel instant; server confirms within 100ms imperceptibly. Reduced motion: no card lift, instant transitions. Color-blind: active player indicated by border thickness + label, not just amber tint.
balance_considerations: Watch desync rate (target <0.1% of actions) — higher = client/server state machine drift, immediate fix. Reconnect success rate target >95% (else disconnect = effective forfeit). Turn timeout rate per game (target <5% of turns time out — else clock too short, frustrating). Action latency p95 < 200ms (else feels laggy). For competitive integrity: track 'rage-quit at losing position' rate, apply LP penalty same as forfeit.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Modified client claiming to play a card not in hand — server validates against authoritative state, action rejected, full state re-pushed.
Submitting actions out-of-turn — server enforces currentPlayer check.
Drawing 'specific' cards by exploiting client-side PRNG — server uses server-seeded PRNG, client never sees seed.
Replay attacks (sending old action packets) — actions tied to turn number, stale packets rejected.
Stalling tactics to time-out opponent's clock — Fischer increment (+5s per move) rewards active play; passive defense still loses to creative attacks.
DDoSing opponent at critical moment — reconnect with 30s grace means single network blip can't decide match.
observability: Log every action with full state-before-hash. Metrics: action latency p50/p95/p99, desync rate, reconnect rate + success, average turn duration, match abandonment rate. Designer dashboard: action validation rejection reasons (catches cheating attempts in patterns), per-card play rate (balance signals).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior trusts client to declare 'I played a 0-mana 10-damage card'. Within 72 hours, scripts exist that play infinite cards. Then: no reconnect, every subway-tunnel disconnect = forfeit, mobile retention collapses. Then: state synced via polling every 5s, feels laggy, players quit for snappier competitors. Then: no chess clock, games can stall forever, ranked queue becomes 30-min waits.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Hearthstone (impeccable reconnect, server-authoritative, replay system), Marvel Snap (1-minute turns + reconnect). DONE BADLY: Diablo Immortal launch — server-authoritative but lossy on disconnect, players lost rare loot drops if disconnected mid-fight; multi-month outcry. Lesson: 'I lost progress to a network blip' is a refund-trigger event.

game_genre_context: Roguelike / dungeon crawler (Slay the Spire, Hades, Diablo) where every run is procedurally generated yet must feel hand-crafted. Bad PCG creates 'random=boring' or 'random=broken'. Slay the Spire's map generation is studied as the gold standard for shareable seed-driven runs.
title: Seeded Roguelike Map Generator with Balance Constraints and Shareable Run IDs
design_intent: Build a dungeon map generator that produces 15-floor maps with branching paths, room types (combat, elite, treasure, shop, event, rest, boss), procedural quest text from templates, and procedurally-named loot ('Ancient Sword of Burning Vengeance'). Seeded so any 'run code' reproduces same content (community challenges). Balance constraints prevent broken combinations (no 3 elites in a row, no shop on floor 1, etc.). Slay the Spire's seed-share feature created an entire competitive scene.
mathematical_foundation: Map: 15 floors × 7 columns of nodes; connections form DAG with branching factor 1-3. Room type weights vary by floor: floors 1-5 favor combats, 6-10 introduce more elites, floor 15 always boss. Markov chain for NPC dialogue: 2nd-order chain trained on hand-authored corpus. Loot name generation: <Adjective> <Material> <Weapon> of <Power> <Element> — 50 × 20 × 30 × 25 × 10 = 7.5M unique names, with rarity tagging based on adjective+suffix combo weights. PRNG: xorshift128+ seeded with player_id ⊕ run_number (so shareable seeds = same map for any player).
psychology_notes: Procedural names feel SPECIFIC even when randomized — players feel like THEY found that unique sword. Seed-share creates community ('try this seed, it's wild'). Branching paths give agency (every choice matters) without overwhelming. Balance constraints prevent unwinnable runs (game designer's job is to make 95% of random runs FUN). Visible room types in map preview let players plan strategy — choice creates investment.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Run starts with player ID + run_number → seed = hash(player_id ⊕ run_number) → seeded PRNG generates 15-floor map structure → for each floor, place rooms via weighted selection respecting constraints → for each room, generate contents (enemy lineup, loot table, event text) deterministically. All deterministic = perfect replays / seed-sharing.

2. **Math**: Connection generation: from each node, choose 1-3 forward connections to next floor. Constraints: no horizontal teleport (must move forward), elite-elite adjacency forbidden, shop-elite adjacency forbidden (can't recover post-elite). Loot rarity from rolled adjective tier — 'Common' adjective + 'Common' suffix = Common; one mid-tier + one high-tier = Rare; both high-tier + rare modifier = Epic.

3. **Edge cases**: Seed produces an unwinnable map (all elites in row 1) — constraint checker rejects, re-roll with seed+1 (deterministic recovery, still shareable). Procedural text comes out grammatically broken — fallback to hand-authored alternates. Loot name collisions (two players get same name) — fine, they're meant to feel unique TO that player but global uniqueness not required.

4. **Visual**: Map shown as connected nodes with iconography per room type. Player position glows. Future rooms previewable but locked (must reach to commit). Loot drop ceremony names item with typewriter animation: each word reveals with rarity color, builds anticipation. Seed shown in pause menu for sharing — copy-to-clipboard button.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Map structure is deterministic from seed, but ENEMY rolls within a room use a separate PRNG stream so 'scouting' via reload doesn't let you predict enemy stats (cheese-prevention). Loot quality similarly. Server validates that observed room sequence matches seed (anti-cheat against modded maps).

6. **Rejected**: Pure random per room (creates broken runs), hand-authored maps only (no replayability), procedurally generated everything including art (current AI not good enough for player-facing assets).

database_design.schema: Postgres runs: id, player_id, seed BIGINT, run_number, started_at, ended_at, floor_reached INT, status. Map structure stored as JSON: nodes array (id, floor, col, room_type), edges array. PCG content tables: adjectives, materials, weapons, suffixes, elements — all with weight + rarity_score columns. Markov chain transitions stored in dialogue_markov: prev_word_pair, next_word, count. Room templates: room_id, floor_min, floor_max, weight, enemy_pool, loot_pool, event_text_template.
database_design.indexes: runs: (player_id, run_number) UNIQUE, (seed) for community lookups. Markov: (prev_word_pair) for fast next-token lookup.
database_design.denormalization: Map structure denormalized into run record as JSON — never recomputed (deterministic from seed but cached for performance).
database_design.transactions: Run creation is single insert with seeded map gen wrapped in retry-on-constraint-fail loop.

visualization_strategy: Map shown as climbing graph (bottom = start, top = boss) — visual journey upward = progress metaphor. Room types color+icon coded (red=combat, purple=elite, gold=treasure, etc.) so players plan at-a-glance. Reachable nodes brightened, unreachable dimmed. Loot reveal: word-by-word typewriter cascade in rarity color with shadow glow — Legendary feels EARNED because of the slow reveal ceremony. Seed prominently displayed for sharing — community gold. Reduced motion: instant reveal, no word cascade. Color-blind: room types use icons AND color, rarity uses border thickness.
balance_considerations: Track per-seed completion rate — if some seeds have <10% win rate vs avg 35%, they're broken (post-balance review). Watch room-type entry distribution: combat should be 50%, elites 10-15%, etc. — if real distribution drifts, generator bugged. Loot rarity distribution: target ~70% common, 20% uncommon, 8% rare, 2% legendary per run. Track seed-share virality (seeds with most plays — community-curated 'good seeds' become marketing).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Map-scouting via save-state reload to see future rooms — server caches map at run-start, client only sees revealed nodes.
Loot reroll exploitation via game crash — loot generated deterministically from seed+room_visit_count; reloading gives same drop.
Seed manipulation to get 'lucky' runs in ranked — ranked mode uses server-generated seeds, never player-chosen.
Markov dialogue generation producing offensive content — output passed through profanity filter; fallback to hand-authored.
Path enumeration to find 'perfect' route via bot — server validates room sequence matches map structure; bot must still play through encounters.
observability: Log map_generated, room_entered, loot_dropped, run_completed. Metrics: per-seed win rate, room visit frequency, loot rarity distribution actual vs target, dialogue generation latency, profanity filter trigger rate. Designer dashboard: 'broken seed' detection, room-type popularity (do players avoid certain types?), loot name 'epic phrasing' tracker (which names go viral on social).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior uses Math.random for map gen — no seed-share community possible, no reproducibility for bug reports. Then: no constraints, generates map with all elites in row 1, player dies, refund. Then: loot names like 'Iron Sword' — boring, no identity attached. Then: Markov chain trained on insufficient corpus, outputs gibberish or worse — offensive combinations. Reddit thread: 'this game's procgen is broken' with screenshots.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Slay the Spire (best-in-class seed-sharing, balanced map gen), Hades (procedural Greek-mythos-themed level layouts), Brogue (legendary procgen depth). DONE BADLY: Daggerfall (1996) — 'thousands of procedurally generated dungeons' that were all bland and empty; technical marvel, gameplay forgettable. Lesson: procedural generation must serve gameplay variety, not exist for its own sake.

game_genre_context: Action FPS / MOBA (Overwatch 2, League of Legends, Apex Legends) where the HUD is the cognitive interface to the entire game. Players check health, cooldowns, minimap, kills/deaths, objective progress — all in peripheral vision while focused on aiming. A cluttered HUD kills focus; an absent HUD leaves players blind. Riot has a team of UX designers solely focused on HUD legibility at 144Hz.
title: Information-Dense HUD with Threshold-Triggered Health Bars, Radial Cooldowns, and Damage Number Juice
design_intent: Build a HUD that COMMUNICATES status through animation, not text. Health bar changes color and pulses at low HP (instant peripheral awareness without breaking focus). Ability cooldowns use radial sweeps so players estimate readiness at-a-glance. Damage numbers float with physics for satisfying feedback. Kill feed animated in from the side, never blocking center. Combo counter for streak feedback. Overwatch's HUD is studied by UX designers; Cyberpunk 2077's launch HUD obscured 30% of the screen with overlapping notifications.
mathematical_foundation: Health bar color: HSL(120 - (1 - hp/max_hp) * 120, 70%, 50%) — green to red as health drops. Pulse rate: pulse_hz = 1 + (1 - hp/max_hp)² * 4 — accelerates as health drops. Damage number physics: position(t) = initial + velocity*t + 0.5*gravity*t² with random horizontal velocity. Radial cooldown angle: angle = (1 - remaining/total) * 360°. Combo decay: combo_value(t) = combo_peak * exp(-t/decay_constant), decay_constant = 3 seconds.
psychology_notes: Color and animation are processed faster than text — at 200ms reactions you can't read 'health: 25/100', but you CAN see 'pulsing red bar'. Audible heartbeat at low HP triggers fight-or-flight subconsciously. Damage numbers create dopamine for every hit landed — Diablo built an empire on satisfying number-pop. Combo counters reward sustained performance, encouraging 'don't break the chain'. Kill feed off to side respects center-screen attention — players can SCAN it during dead time.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Game state (HP, abilities, kills) syncs via WebSocket every 50ms or on change. Client renders HUD with React + canvas overlay for high-FPS damage numbers. Threshold-triggered effects (low HP pulse, kill streak) listen to state diffs and fire animations.

2. **Math**: Health-bar HSL interpolation is perceptually more natural than RGB lerp (linear hue rotation green→yellow→red). Pulse rate accelerating with damage creates 'rising panic' physiology match. Damage number physics simple kinematics — feels weighty, not floaty.

3. **Edge cases**: Damage burst (50 sources hit at once) — pool damage numbers into a single '+1250' summary every 250ms to prevent screen pollution. HP regenerates back above threshold — smoothly transition out of pulse without jarring stop. Player has 4 abilities, all on cooldown — radial display shows whichever readies first as most visible.

4. **Visual**: Health bar at top-left for self (peripheral vision sweet spot). Ability bar at bottom-center (action-zone). Minimap top-right (least-glanced corner). Kill feed top-right edge, slide in/out. Combo counter centered when active, fades when broken. Damage numbers in 3D world-space (anchored to victim), then float up + fade.

5. **Anti-exploit**: HUD is read-only display, no exploit vectors — but values driven by server state, never trust client claims of 'my HP is 100'.

6. **Rejected**: Numeric-only health (slower perception), HUD permanently in center (blocks aim), no damage numbers (loss of feedback), text-based kill feed in long form (cognitive overload).

database_design.schema: Most HUD data is real-time, not persisted. Postgres player_settings: player_id, hud_scale, hud_layout JSONB, color_blind_mode ENUM(off, deuteranopia, protanopia, tritanopia), reduce_motion BOOLEAN. Recent damage stored in Redis for kill-cam replay.
database_design.indexes: player_settings: (player_id) PK.
database_design.denormalization: Settings cached on client; only updated on save.
database_design.transactions: Settings update is single document/row update, no transaction needed.

visualization_strategy: Health bar uses HSL interpolation (green→yellow→red) — perceptually smooth, not jarring color jumps. Low HP triggers opacity pulse accelerating with damage — physiological match to player rising panic. Ability cooldowns use radial sweep (universal language from Blizzard/Riot games — instantly readable). Damage numbers float with horizontal scatter — feels physical. Crit damage 1.6x larger + yellow + exclamation — instant 'big hit' read. Kill feed slides in from right, never centered — respects gameplay focus. Color-blind mode: red health becomes patterned, ability ready indicated by glow not just border color. Reduced motion: pulse becomes static low-opacity glow; damage numbers fade in place rather than float.
balance_considerations: Watch HUD-element opt-out rate per setting — if 20% of players hide the kill feed, it's too aggressive. Watch click-through rate from low-HP-event to retreat-action — high = HUD comms working. Track 'mistakes correlated with HUD obstruction' (e.g., death from missed minimap ping) — design for peripheral readability. A/B test damage number physics (slow float vs fast pop) — measure session length.
exploit_vectors_addressed: HUD modification to show enemy info (wallhacks) — game data piped to HUD never includes hidden enemies; client never receives 'enemy at X' until in line-of-sight per server.
Damage number scraping for DPS calc bots — values broadcast are actual gameplay (no leakage), bot exists outside game logic.
UI element overlap exploits — server gameplay continues regardless of HUD state, no HUD-bug griefing possible.
observability: Log hud_settings_changed, hud_element_hidden, mode_switched (color-blind/reduce-motion). Metrics: avg session length by HUD configuration, % using accessibility modes, complaint rate per element. Designer dashboard: heatmap of player eye-attention (if eye-tracking available) by HUD region.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior uses static red bar with '23/100 HP' text. Player at 200ms reaction can't read text mid-fight, dies, frustration. Then: no damage numbers, hits feel weightless, players say game 'feels mushy'. Then: ability cooldowns shown as text '4.3s' — distracting, no peripheral awareness. Then: kill feed centered — blocks aim, players rage-quit. Cyberpunk 2077 launch HUD had this exact problem.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Overwatch (Blizzard UX team studied frame-by-frame), League of Legends (information density mastery), Diablo (damage number juice that built an empire). DONE BADLY: Cyberpunk 2077 launch — overlapping HUD elements that covered 30% of screen, accessibility issues (no color-blind mode at launch), kill notifications blocking center vision. Patched over months. Lesson: HUD is a UX discipline, not an art-asset task.

game_genre_context: Web-based collectible game (Marvel Snap web mode, NFT card games, Hearthstone Battlegrounds web companion). Players collect cards/heroes with 3D rendering for showcase moments. The 3D collection screen IS the marketing material — screenshots shared on social drive new acquisitions.
title: 3D Card Flip + Holographic Foil Shader with Instanced Particle Burst on Reveal
design_intent: Build a 3D card showcase with: physics-based flip animation, custom shader for holographic foil effect on rare cards (the secondary refractor effect on real-world Pokemon cards), instanced particle burst on legendary reveal, and 60fps performance on mid-range mobile browsers. The 'foil' effect is what makes screenshots shareable — players post these on Twitter when they pull rares. Hearthstone's legendary card-back animation and Marvel Snap's 3D card art are the standard.
mathematical_foundation: Card flip: smooth interpolation via easing function — bezier(0.25, 0.8, 0.25, 1) for material-design-like spring. Foil shader: view-dependent diffraction simulated via interference pattern based on tan(uv * frequency + dot(normal, viewDir) * shift). Particle burst: 200 instanced quads emitted along golden-ratio spiral (φ = (1 + √5)/2 ≈ 1.618), with random velocity perturbation. Camera lerp on card hover: rotation = clamp(mouse_offset * sensitivity, -0.3, 0.3) radians.
psychology_notes: Card flip + foil shimmer creates 'I have a treasure' identity — collectibles must FEEL precious. Holographic effect mimics real-world rare-card iconography from childhood (Pokemon foil cards) — instant nostalgia. Particle burst on legendary reveal triggers dopamine spike. Slow, ceremonial reveal slows time, creates moment for screenshot/share. Cards displayed in 3D feel like objects you own, not pixels in inventory.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Card data fetched from server (with rarity, art_url, foil_pattern_seed). 3D component renders card mesh in @react-three/fiber, applies foil shader if rarity >= rare. Reveal animation triggered on first display. Camera responds to mouse position. On rarity >= legendary, particle system instanced burst plays. Screenshot button captures Three.js canvas to PNG.

2. **Math**: Foil shader: interference pattern computed in fragment shader, view-direction dependent for parallax shift. Particle emission angles use golden ratio spiral for visually 'organic' distribution (looks better than uniform random). Card flip uses quaternion slerp not Euler interpolation (avoids gimbal lock at 90°).

3. **Edge cases**: WebGL context lost (mobile background tab) — listen for context-lost event, restore gracefully. Mobile device with weak GPU — fallback to flat texture without shader effects (auto-detected via WEBGL_renderer info). Reduce-motion preference — skip flip animation, instant card display.

4. **Visual**: Card resting state has slight idle sway (subtle, breathes life). Hover: subtle tilt toward mouse, foil pattern shifts as if catching light. Reveal: card spins from back to front with motion blur, particles burst on legendary. Background dimmed during reveal — focus.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Card data server-validated; rarity not client-controllable. Foil pattern seeded from player_id + card_instance to prevent re-roll exploitation.

6. **Rejected**: 2D card with PNG foil overlay (not as impressive), GIF foil (huge bandwidth, no view-dependent effect), pre-rendered video (no interactivity).

database_design.schema: Cards table: id, name, art_url, mesh_url, rarity, foil_enabled BOOLEAN. PlayerCards: player_id, card_id, instance_id (unique per pull), foil_seed BIGINT, acquired_at.
database_design.indexes: PlayerCards: (player_id, card_id), (instance_id) UNIQUE.
database_design.denormalization: Card metadata cached on client after first load (CDN-hostable JSON).
database_design.transactions: Card acquisition is single insert; idempotency on instance_id.

visualization_strategy: Card hover responds to mouse — feels held, not pinned. Foil shader's intensity scales with rarity (0 for common, 1 for legendary) — visual rarity-ladder. Particle burst only for legendary — preserves specialness (over-using particle effects devalues them). Camera lerp uses spring not linear — feels heavy, weighty. Reduced motion: card displays flat, no spin/sway. Color-blind safe: foil color uses warm-to-cool gradient (not problematic hue range). Screenshot mode: snapshots Three.js canvas to PNG for sharing.
balance_considerations: Watch screenshot-share rate per rarity — legendary should be 10x rare. If particle burst doesn't drive shares, polish the moment more. Watch FPS on lower-end devices — target sustained 60fps on 2020-era mobile; if drops to 30fps, auto-fallback to 2D rendering. A/B test foil intensity (more vs less) on conversion.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Modifying foil_seed client-side to fake legendary appearance — server is single source for foil_seed; client display is purely cosmetic.
Extracting card art assets — art_url returns watermarked low-res; full-res only renders in shader (extraction would require Three.js scraping, still watermarked).
Forging 'rarity' display to claim legendary on social — rarity tied to server-verified card instance, screenshot ≠ ownership claim.
observability: Log card_revealed (with rarity), screenshot_taken, share_clicked, fps_dropped (below 30). Metrics: avg FPS by device class, screenshot share rate per rarity, time spent on card showcase screen (engagement signal).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior uses static PNG with hover-effect glow. Players never feel the 'rare' moment, no shares, low engagement. Then: 2D animation on legendary reveal, players don't screenshot, marketing has no organic content. Then: shader works on desktop, crashes on iOS Safari (WebGL quirks), 30% of mobile users see fallback or worse. Then: foil effect over-applied to commons, devalues legendaries, players complain 'every card looks the same'.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Marvel Snap 3D cards (excellent foil shaders, performant on mobile), Hearthstone legendary card backs (memorable reveal animations), Pokemon TCG Live (faithful holographic recreation). DONE BADLY: Many NFT 'collectible' web apps shipped with low-effort static images, no juice — collections feel like spreadsheets, players churn. Lesson: collectibles need ceremony, or they're just data.

game_genre_context: Mid-core social MMO or strategy game (Clash of Clans clans, Lords Mobile guilds, EVE Online corporations) where guild membership IS retention. Solo players churn at 70% in 30 days; guild members churn at 25%. The guild is the most important player-retention mechanic in the entire game.
title: Guild System with Hierarchical Permissions, Collective Quests, and Chat Moderation Tools
design_intent: Build a guild system with Leader/Officer/Member roles, collective guild quests (every member contributes), guild XP and level progression, guild-only chat with moderation tools (slow mode, word filters, report queue), and anti-poaching (gifting limits). Clash of Clans built a $10B+ empire on this exact pattern. The 'guild chat with friends' loop is monetization's secret — players don't want to leave their friends.
mathematical_foundation: Guild XP per member contribution: log-scaled by member count to prevent mega-guilds dominating: contrib_xp_modifier = 1 / log2(2 + member_count). Guild level XP curve: xp_for_level(L) = 1000 * pow(L, 1.5). Collective quest progress: each member's contribution capped at 25% of target (prevents one whale solo-completing). Chat slow mode: rate-limit message_per_minute = base_rate / (1 + recent_messages_count / window). Moderation auto-action: report_threshold = 3 unique reporters within 24h → auto-mute.
psychology_notes: Tribal identity is the most powerful retention force in F2P games. Players quit ranked, quit ladder, quit dailies — they don't quit their friends. Hierarchy (Leader/Officer/Member) creates achievement vectors WITHIN the guild (becoming officer is a goal). Collective quests force coordination — coordinating with humans creates emotional investment. Guild chat is the friendship engine; mute-and-ban tools make it tolerable to be in a guild even with one toxic person.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player creates guild (cost gems, prevents spam guilds) or applies to one. Leader/officer approves. Joins guild chat, sees guild quest progress, can contribute. Daily/weekly guild quests track collective progress. Guild leveling unlocks perks (member cap, vault size, etc.). Moderation actions logged with reason; appeals possible.

2. **Math**: Contribution XP scaling prevents one whale carrying 50-person guild (kills mid-tier player engagement). Log-scale guild member modifier means a 5-person guild's quest is achievable by activity, not just headcount. Auto-mute threshold of 3 reports balances false-positive risk vs response speed.

3. **Edge cases**: Guild leader stops playing — auto-succession after 14 days inactive (next-highest officer promoted, or member with most contribution if no officer). Mass-leave (raid by competitor poaching) — guild remains intact, no auto-disband. Player banned from game still has guild record — preserved but unable to interact, frees up slot. Chat spam attack from bots — rate-limited per-user AND per-guild.

4. **Visual**: Guild dashboard with member list (sorted by activity/contribution), level progress bar, quest progress visualized as collective bar (player's own contribution highlighted). Chat with mod buttons accessible to officers+. Member contribution leaderboard (gentle competition within guild).

5. **Anti-exploit**: Guild creation costs gems (anti-spam). Gifting between members rate-limited (anti-RMT). Multi-account same-guild detected via device fingerprint + behavior (one person running 5 alts for collective quest = ban). Chat profanity filter + manual review queue.

6. **Rejected**: Flat hierarchy (no role-based achievement), unlimited gifting (RMT bait), no moderation tools (toxic chat = guild dies), permanent leader (dead-leader problem).

database_design.schema: Postgres guilds: id, name UNIQUE, tag UNIQUE, level INT, xp BIGINT, member_cap INT, created_by, created_at, description TEXT, language TEXT. guild_members: guild_id, player_id, role ENUM(leader, officer, member, applicant), joined_at, last_contribution_at, total_contribution BIGINT. guild_quests: id, guild_id, def_id, progress BIGINT, target BIGINT, expires_at, status. guild_quest_contributions: quest_id, player_id, contribution BIGINT. guild_chat_messages: id, guild_id, sender_id, content TEXT, deleted BOOLEAN, deleted_by, deleted_reason, created_at. guild_moderation_reports: id, guild_id, reporter_id, target_id, message_id, reason, status, created_at.
database_design.indexes: guild_members: (player_id), (guild_id, role), (guild_id, last_contribution_at DESC). guild_chat_messages: (guild_id, created_at DESC). guild_moderation_reports: (target_id, created_at DESC), (status, created_at).
database_design.denormalization: Guild summary (level, member count) cached on client. Player.guild_id denormalized for fast lookups.
database_design.transactions: Member promotion/demotion: lock both rows, update roles, log audit. Quest contribution: lock quest row, increment progress + per-player contribution; reward grant on completion uses transaction.

visualization_strategy: Role badges color-coded (gold=leader, violet=officer, slate=member) — instant hierarchy scan. Quest bar shows TOTAL progress (cyan) and MY contribution (yellow overlay) — personal stake visible within collective goal. Member list sorted by contribution — gentle pressure to contribute without shaming. Chat shows moderation actions on hover (officers+ only) — power available, not aggressive. Color-blind: role indicated by badge + label. Reduced motion: progress bars instant fill.
balance_considerations: Watch guild member retention vs solo retention (target 3x gap). Watch contribution Gini within guilds — high inequality means leader carries, others don't engage (rebalance contribution caps). Watch moderation report volume per guild — high = toxic guild, intervention worthwhile. Watch active-member-percentage of guild cap — guilds at 80% utilization are healthy, <40% are dead.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Multi-account farming guild quests — device fingerprint + behavior analysis catches alt-account farms; one human can't realistically play 5 accounts simultaneously without detection.
Toxic chat harassment — auto-mute on 3+ unique reporters within 24h; profanity filter + manual review escalation.
Spam-creating guilds to launder reputation — guild creation costs 500 gems; old guilds with dead leader auto-succession prevents 'shell guilds'.
RMT via guild gifting — gift volume rate-limited per pair; unusual gifting patterns flagged.
Officer abuse (mass-kick to dissolve guild) — kick action rate-limited per officer per day; mass-kick triggers leader notification.
Poaching via competing guild leader DMing all members — chat names limited to guild-public; no PMs from non-friends by default.
observability: Log guild_created, joined, left, kicked, quest_contribution, message_sent, message_reported, mute_applied. Metrics: guild retention curve, member churn rate by role, quest completion rate by member count, moderation action rate, chat message volume. Designer dashboard: 'guild health' score (active member %, contribution Gini, moderation flags) — guilds at risk of collapse predicted from these.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior implements guild as just a chat room. No quests, no levels, no roles. Players bored, leave in 7 days. Then: no moderation, one toxic player ruins guild for 30 others, they all quit. Then: no leader succession, top guilds have inactive leaders for months, can't kick non-contributing members, decay. Then: gifting unlimited, RMT website appears within 2 weeks selling 'in-game gold via guild gift'.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Clash of Clans (clan system carried the game 10+ years), Lords Mobile (alliance mechanics are the entire metagame). DONE BADLY: Anthem launch — strongholds had no guild/clan system AT ALL, social retention nonexistent, game died in 8 months. EA cited lack of social features in postmortem. Lesson: solo retention is half the curve; guild retention is the rest.

game_genre_context: Competitive PvP shooter or MOBA where one cheater per lobby destroys 9 other players' experience. Riot's Vanguard, Valve's VAC, EAC (Easy Anti-Cheat) — billion-dollar problems. Cheaters drive 5x more churn than any other factor. Detection must be both real-time (kick from current match) and post-hoc (ban accounts).
title: Statistical Anti-Cheat with Shadow-Banning and Replay-Based Hardware Fingerprinting
design_intent: Build an anti-cheat layer that detects cheaters via statistical anomaly detection on gameplay actions (impossible aim, wall-shooting, perfect economy), shadow-bans cheaters into segregated matches (they think they're winning, real players never face them), and uses behavioral fingerprinting to detect alt-account ban evasion. Real bans risk false positives that destroy trust — shadow bans don't. Trust Factor (Counter-Strike) and Hidden MMR pools (Overwatch) are the gold standard.
mathematical_foundation: Aim anomaly: per-shot, compute angle between crosshair-pre-shot and target-vector-post-shot. Aimbots show distribution with sharp peak near 0° + occasional jitter; humans show Gaussian centered ~3-5°. Use Kolmogorov-Smirnov test against baseline distribution. Wallhack detection: tracks pre-aim accuracy (turning toward enemy BEFORE line-of-sight) — humans pre-aim 5-15% based on map knowledge; wallhackers pre-aim 40%+. Behavioral fingerprint: 50-dim vector of mouse-acceleration curve features, keystroke timing, action patterns. Same player across accounts gives cosine similarity > 0.85.
psychology_notes: Direct bans tell cheaters their cheat detected — they iterate and come back. Shadow bans waste their time without learning. Visible bans on cheaters' friends create paranoia in the cheater community — 'they're watching' is more deterring than 'they catch some of us'. False-positive trauma is real — wrongly banning a legit player is the worst outcome; better to under-detect.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Match server emits per-action telemetry (shot fired, hit/miss, crosshair pos, target pos, time-to-aim-on-enemy, recoil pattern). Real-time analyzer flags suspicious patterns mid-match for high-confidence cases (impossible aim) → mid-match kick. Post-match deeper analysis with statistical tests on full match data → confidence score. >0.9 score = shadow ban; 0.7-0.9 = manual review queue; <0.7 = clear.

2. **Math**: Multiple weak signals combined via logistic regression on labeled training data (manual review confirms). Behavioral fingerprint cosine similarity for alt-account detection — if banned account had fingerprint F, any new account showing >0.85 similarity within 30 days flagged.

3. **Edge cases**: Pro player with insanely good aim (Faker, s1mple) — manually whitelisted, statistical anomaly score capped from triggering auto-ban. New account uploading replay of recent banned account match = same fingerprint = ban-evasion detected. Cheat-coaching (legit player imitating cheats to investigate report) — flagged for review, not auto-banned.

4. **Visual**: Cheaters don't see ban notification — just matched with fewer players, queue takes longer (subtle). Reports get vague 'thanks for reporting' — never reveal whether action taken (prevents probing). Honest players get 'a player you reported was actioned' notification (closes loop, builds trust).

5. **Anti-exploit**: Statistical thresholds re-tuned monthly against new cheat patterns. Manual review queue for borderline cases — ML output + human verification. Appeal process exists but with very high bar (must prove false positive).

6. **Rejected**: Kernel-level anti-cheat (Vanguard-style) for web games (impossible to implement). Trust-the-client validation (cheaters mod client). Public ban announcements (gives cheat-makers a hint list).

database_design.schema: Postgres player_telemetry: match_id, player_id, action_type, payload JSONB, ts TIMESTAMPTZ. anti_cheat_scores: player_id, match_id, signal_type, value NUMERIC, computed_at. player_behavioral_fingerprint: player_id PK, vector NUMERIC[50], updated_at. ban_records: id, player_id, type ENUM(soft, shadow, hard), reason, evidence JSONB, applied_at, expires_at. shadow_ban_pool: player_id (in this set = matched only with each other).
database_design.indexes: player_telemetry: hypertable on ts, (player_id, ts DESC). anti_cheat_scores: (player_id, signal_type, value DESC) — high-confidence first. fingerprint: GIN-style index for nearest-neighbor (use pgvector extension).
database_design.denormalization: Shadow-ban list in Redis SET for fast match-allocation filtering.
database_design.transactions: Ban application is single update + audit log entry — atomic.

visualization_strategy: Report flow: clear, calm, low-friction (3 clicks max). Categories use rose color (not red — too aggressive). 'Action taken' notification is the trust-builder — honest players need to know the system works. Cheaters get NO feedback that they were detected (shadow ban invisible). Report button hidden under hover to avoid trigger-happy mass reporting. Color-blind: report button uses icon + label. Reduced motion: instant modal open.
balance_considerations: Watch false-positive rate from manual review (target <0.1%). Watch shadow-ban pool size growth (rising = either cheating epidemic or threshold too tight). Watch report-rate per match (>1 per match = either toxicity or actual cheating epidemic). Watch 'action taken' notification CTR — if 0%, players aren't seeing the loop close. Watch cheater detection latency: time from cheat behavior to shadow-ban (target <3 matches).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Aimbot — statistical detection on aim angle distribution (KS test against human baseline).
Wallhack — pre-aim rate detection (looking at enemies through walls before LoS).
Speed-hack — server-authoritative simulation, client position rejected if exceeds physics.
Account sharing for boosting — behavioral fingerprint detects different humans playing same account.
Ban evasion via new account — behavioral fingerprint cosine similarity to recently banned (>0.85 = same human).
Mass-reporting weaponization — unique-reporter threshold + manual review prevents single-player smear.
Cheat-detection probing (cheater testing to see what gets caught) — shadow ban gives no signal, cheater wastes time.
observability: Log every telemetry event, ks_score, ban_applied, report_filed, manual_review. Metrics: cheat detection rate, false positive rate, shadow-ban pool size, ban evasion attempts caught, time-to-detection. Designer dashboard: cheat-pattern emergence (new statistical anomaly profiles trending — points to new cheat tool in wild).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior writes 'if hit rate > 95% ban'. Pro players get banned, refund storm, community trust shattered. Then: cheaters notice their accounts banned, create new ones in 2 minutes, no fingerprinting catches them, they're back in the queue. Then: visible bans give cheat-makers exact feedback on detection, iteration cycle accelerates. Then: no shadow ban, real players see cheaters in lobby every match, quit ranked entirely.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Counter-Strike's Trust Factor (multi-signal, shadow-pool effect), Overwatch's hidden 'low priority queue' for toxic players. DONE BADLY: PUBG launch — minimal anti-cheat, China-based cheating epidemic reached 30%+ of lobbies in 2018, Western retention collapsed, eventually region-locked then partially recovered. Lesson: anti-cheat is product, not infrastructure — invest from day one.

game_genre_context: Action RPG (Diablo IV, Path of Exile, Last Epoch) where loot is the entire core loop — kill monsters, get loot, equip better, kill bigger monsters. Item drops with random affixes are the dopamine engine. Diablo III's launch loot system (Auction House era) is a famous cautionary tale.
title: Affix-Based Item Generator with Item Power Curves and Vendor Trash Filter
design_intent: Build an action-RPG loot generator: items roll with 1-6 affixes from weighted pools, item power scales with monster level via formula, vendor-trash auto-flagged so inventory isn't drowned. Path of Exile's affix system gives nearly infinite item identity. Diablo III's launch flooded players with garbage drops + Auction House — players just bought best items, never felt accomplishment. The fix (Loot 2.0 with smart drops) saved the franchise.
mathematical_foundation: Item power = monster_level * (1 + variance), variance ~Normal(0, 0.05). Affix count distribution: P(1)=0.3, P(2)=0.35, P(3)=0.2, P(4)=0.1, P(5)=0.04, P(6)=0.01 (6-affix is mythical). Affix value: rolled within tier ranges, tier weight ∝ 1/tier^1.5 (lower tiers more common). Vendor-trash threshold: items where all affixes are bottom-tier AND no synergy bonuses. Item rarity tied to affix count: 1-2 = Common, 3 = Magic, 4 = Rare, 5 = Epic, 6 = Legendary.
psychology_notes: Variable affix combinations create 'is THIS one the chase item?' tension on every drop. Identification step (Diablo's classic 'unidentified' state) extends anticipation. Item names communicate rarity at a glance. Vendor-trash filter prevents inventory burnout (most-cited complaint in Diablo III launch). Affix synergies (movement speed + skill cooldown on same item) create 'perfect roll' chase content — players run same content for months hunting one item.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Monster killed → loot table for monster determines item base + roll seed. Server rolls affix count, affix selection per pool with weights, affix values within tiers. Item created with full metadata. Vendor-trash flag applied if all bottom-tier. Pushed to player inventory.

2. **Math**: Affix tier weights of 1/tier^1.5 chosen because pure exponential drops too fast (T1 dominates), linear too uniform (T5 not rare enough). 1.5 power gives nice geometric feel. Item power variance of 5% keeps drops feeling individual but predictable.

3. **Edge cases**: Player at level 80 farming level 30 zone — items rolled at monster level, not player level (or you'd farm low-level for guaranteed drops with high stats). Inventory full — items drop on ground for 5 min, then despawn (auto-pickup if vendor trash skipped). Duplicate affixes — within an item, affixes from same pool can't roll twice.

4. **Visual**: Drop animations: white text for vendor trash, blue for magic, yellow for rare, orange for legendary, green for unique. Legendary drops play a sound + particle burst. Auto-pickup for white/blue if vendor-trash filter on. Identifying item = brief animation revealing affixes one at a time.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Loot generated server-side with seeded PRNG per drop. Item modification (reroll affixes) costs resources, server-validated. Trading rate-limited to prevent dupe exploits.

6. **Rejected**: Items with hardcoded stat ranges (no variety), every monster drops same loot table regardless of level (boring), inventory infinite (clutter overwhelms).

database_design.schema: Postgres item_bases: id, name, item_type, slot, base_stats JSONB, affix_pools JSONB. affix_definitions: id, code, pool_id, tier INT, weight NUMERIC, stat_id, min_value, max_value. player_inventory: id, player_id, base_id, affixes JSONB[], item_power INT, identified BOOLEAN, rarity, vendor_trash BOOLEAN, generated_seed BIGINT, created_at.
database_design.indexes: player_inventory: (player_id, vendor_trash, rarity DESC, item_power DESC) for sorted views. affix_definitions: (pool_id, tier, weight).
database_design.denormalization: Affix tier ranges denormalized into item_inventory at generation (so future affix def changes don't retroactively alter existing items).
database_design.transactions: Item generation is single insert. Reroll is transaction: deduct currency, update affixes, increment version.

visualization_strategy: Rarity color universal: white < blue < yellow < purple < orange — Diablo-canon color literacy. Glow intensity scales with rarity (legendaries have screen-relevant glow). Drop popup floats up + fades (acknowledged but not blocking). Tooltip uses item border color + glow for instant rarity recognition. 'Sell trash' button only appears when trash exists — no clutter. Color-blind: rarity shown as icon (1-5 stars) alongside color. Reduced motion: pop-in becomes fade, no glow animation.
balance_considerations: Watch drop rate distribution — should match design (70% common, 22% magic, 6% rare, 1.8% epic, 0.2% legendary). If real drift, generator bug. Watch player time-per-loot-decision (target <5s for trash, <20s for rare+). Watch inventory full rate (target <10% of sessions). Watch reroll mechanic engagement — too cheap = whales reroll perfect items easily; too expensive = no engagement.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Save-scumming for perfect rolls — item generation deterministic from server-seed, can't be re-rolled by reload.
Inventory exploit (deleting then 'finding' item via desync) — server is source of truth, never trusts client claim.
Affix injection via API manipulation — affix values validated against generation seed; impossible affix values rejected at trade.
Dupe via simultaneous trade — trade transactions atomic with row locks.
Multi-account farming — accounts on same device tracked via fingerprint, suspicious patterns flagged.
observability: Log every item_generated, identified, sold, equipped, traded. Metrics: rarity distribution, affix combination popularity, items-per-hour by player level, vendor-trash-ratio per zone, identify rate vs auto-sell rate. Designer dashboard: 'chase items' — which affix combinations players keep instead of selling.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior generates items with hardcoded affix combinations (no real variety) — players see same items, lose 'chase'. Then: no vendor-trash filter, inventory fills with junk, players quit out of frustration. Then: drops are linear power (no rarity gradient), legendaries feel same as commons. Then: item generation uses client-side Math.random — players save-scum to get perfect rolls in 5 minutes, item value collapses, hardcore quits. Diablo 3 launch had AH-related variant of this disaster.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Path of Exile (vast affix pool, deep crafting), Diablo IV post-launch (Loot 2.0 lessons applied). DONE BADLY: Diablo III launch (2012) — Auction House dominated loot acquisition, drops felt meaningless, expansion required removal of AH to save franchise. Lesson: loot must FEEL earned through play, not bought via market.

game_genre_context: Mobile idle / farming game (Hay Day, Township, AFK Arena) where daily check-in IS the gameplay. Players play 5 minutes a day, daily quests are 80% of session time. Resets, streaks, and grace periods are existential.
title: Streak System with Multi-Day Grace Period and Compound Reward Tiers
design_intent: Build a daily login + quest streak system where missing a single day DOESN'T break a 47-day streak — players get a 'grace token' to backfill one missed day per 7-day period. Streak rewards scale exponentially (day 30 ≫ day 7), and visiting the app within 24h of last visit refreshes streak. Duolingo perfected this — their 'streak freeze' became iconic. AFK Arena's daily checkpoint with streak counter is the mobile gaming version. The streak IS the retention mechanism.
mathematical_foundation: Streak length S. Reward at day D: R(D) = base * (1.15)^min(D, 30) — exponential up to day 30, plateaus to prevent infinite scaling. Grace token earned: 1 every 7 days of unbroken streak, max 2 stored. Visit window: 24h-36h since last claim = streak continues; 36h-48h = grace token auto-applied (if available); >48h or grace exhausted = streak resets. Compound bonus at milestones (7, 14, 30, 60, 100 days): bonus chest with rare items.
psychology_notes: Loss aversion is the most powerful retention force — a 47-day streak feels like an asset. Breaking it = identity loss. Grace tokens convert 'I lost my streak from one bad day, I quit' into 'whew, dodged that, still going'. Duolingo's research showed streak freeze items doubled 30-day retention. Milestone bonuses give large-magnitude moments amid the small-daily rhythm.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player opens app → server checks last_claim_at vs now → within 24h: streak unchanged (already claimed today). 24-36h: streak continues, claim today, grant reward. 36-48h: streak continues IF grace token available (auto-consumed). >48h or no grace: streak reset to 1.

2. **Math**: Exponential 1.15^D feels rewarding without becoming insane (day 30 reward = 66x base — that's the 'I have to keep this going' threshold). Plateau at D=30 prevents whales from having 365-day streaks worth millions. Grace token earn-rate of 1 per 7 days is permissive without trivializing the streak.

3. **Edge cases**: Time-zone change between visits — uses elapsed time, not calendar date, so traveling doesn't break streak. Server clock off — use NTP-synced server time only. Phone uninstall + reinstall within 24h — server tracks per account, not device. Multi-device — claim is once per account per window.

4. **Visual**: Streak counter prominent on home screen. Calendar view showing past 30 days with claimed/grace-used/missed indicators. Milestone celebrations at 7/14/30/etc. Grace token icon with count, used animation when auto-applied. 'Streak in danger' warning if approaching 36h window.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Server-time only. Claim rate-limited to 1 per 24h. Grace tokens server-tracked.

6. **Rejected**: Streak breaks on single missed day (high churn), unlimited grace (kills the asset feeling), reward linear (no late-game motivation).

database_design.schema: Postgres player_streak: player_id PK, current_streak INT, longest_streak INT, last_claim_at TIMESTAMPTZ, grace_tokens INT, grace_earned_total INT, total_claims INT. streak_claim_log: id, player_id, day INT, claim_at, reward_payload, used_grace BOOLEAN.
database_design.indexes: player_streak: (player_id) PK. streak_claim_log: (player_id, claim_at DESC).
database_design.denormalization: player.current_streak denormalized to player record for UI.
database_design.transactions: Claim is single tx: lock player_streak row, validate elapsed time, update fields, insert log, grant reward.

visualization_strategy: Flame icon scales with streak length and changes color (yellow→orange→amber→red — temperature mapping). Pulses gently always; pulses faster + turns red when streak in danger (peripheral awareness). Grace tokens shown as shield icon with count — clearly defensive item. Calendar shows last 7 days at glance — immediate 'I've been good' satisfaction. Milestone celebrations are full-screen with confetti — earned moments. Reduced motion: static flame, no pulse. Color-blind: streak intensity also indicated by flame icon size, not just color.
balance_considerations: Watch 30/60/90-day retention by streak presence — players with active streaks should retain 3-4x better. Watch grace token usage rate — if >40% of claims use grace, grace is too generous (or app is too punishing). Watch streak-break rate at exactly day 30 (post-plateau) — if drops here, post-plateau reward needs to feel ongoing. A/B test 24h vs 36h grace window — longer = more saves, shorter = more loss-aversion pressure.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Client-time manipulation to fake daily logins — server time only.
Multi-account 'streak farming' — claim tied to account, devices don't earn separately.
Grace token duplication via crash — generation and consumption atomic in transaction.
Streak claim race condition (two parallel API calls) — SELECT FOR UPDATE prevents double-claim.
observability: Log claim events with streak length, grace used, milestone. Metrics: average streak length by cohort, retention curve segmented by current streak, grace usage frequency, milestone hit rate. Designer dashboard: 'streak length distribution' (healthy = right-skewed, broken = bunched at low days).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: streak = consecutive 24h windows, missed = reset. Player on 47-day streak has flight delay, can't open app for 30h, streak gone. Quits app, 1-star review, viral Twitter post. Then: no danger warning — player wakes up to broken streak, 'where was my reminder?'. Then: linear rewards — day 30 worth same as day 5, no incentive to maintain.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Duolingo (streak freeze became iconic, 'don't break the streak' is a meme), Snapchat snapstreak (drove teen retention for years). DONE BADLY: Early MMOs that had 'daily login' with hard-reset, no recovery — players who took vacation lost months of progress, came back to find guild had moved on. Lesson: forgive small lapses, the long arc retains.

game_genre_context: Casual puzzle game (Candy Crush, Wordle, 2048) where achievements provide long-tail goals after main puzzles are mastered. Achievements turn 'I finished the game' into '47% achievements unlocked, plenty to do'.
title: Hidden Achievement Discovery System with Hint Tiers and Anti-Spoiler UI
design_intent: Build a hidden achievement system where players discover secret achievements through gameplay, with progressive hint tiers (after X attempts on a level, show vague hint; after Y, show specific hint). Anti-spoiler UI prevents players who don't want hints from seeing them. Returnal does this well (datacubes give vague lore hints); Fez took it to the extreme with cipher-decoding community puzzles. Hidden achievements = community engagement = wiki traffic = retention.
mathematical_foundation: Discovery probability per attempt: P(discover|attempt=n) increases sigmoidally based on hint tier. Hint reveal thresholds: tier 1 at attempt 5, tier 2 at attempt 15, tier 3 at attempt 30. Decay of hint relevance: hint shown only if last seen >24h ago AND player hasn't dismissed it permanently. Achievement rarity tracked as fraction of total players unlocked, displayed only AFTER unlock.
psychology_notes: Hidden achievements create curiosity ('what unlocked THIS?') and community ('wait, you unlocked Cosmic Glimpse? HOW?'). Spoiler avoidance respects player autonomy. Hint tiers prevent unrealistic difficulty without trivializing — players who want to figure out themselves can, those who need help get it. Discovery feels SPECIAL when achievement was hidden.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Game events still trigger normal achievement check. Hidden achievements have same definition but `hidden: true`. Player sees only locked silhouette in achievements list. Trigger condition checked silently. On unlock: dramatic reveal animation. Hint system: if player attempts the level 5+ times without unlocking, tier 1 hint becomes available (opt-in).

2. **Math**: Hint tiers scale with attempt count, not time — respects player pace. Sigmoid for hint relevance matches player frustration curve.

3. **Edge cases**: Player completes achievement on first try without knowing it existed — retroactive unlock with extra celebration ('AMAZING — you found Cosmic Glimpse blind!'). Player skips hint despite opting in — fine, no progress lock. Multiple hidden achievements involve same level — hints don't conflict (each tracked separately).

4. **Visual**: Hidden achievements show as `???` in list with a question mark icon. Hint UI: 'Want a hint? You've tried this 12 times' button — opt-in, never forced. Unlock celebration extra dramatic for hidden (full screen, dramatic music).

5. **Anti-exploit**: Conditions still server-validated. No exploit incentive on hidden achievements — they're purely cosmetic.

6. **Rejected**: Show hidden achievements as obvious 'secret achievement' (kills mystery), force hints (kills discovery), require community wiki (excludes casual players).

database_design.schema: Extends AchievementDef with hidden: Boolean and hints: [{ tier_threshold INT, text TEXT }]. PlayerAchievementHints: { player_id, achievement_code, attempts INT, hint_tier_unlocked INT, hint_dismissed BOOLEAN, last_shown_at }.
database_design.indexes: PlayerAchievementHints: (player_id, achievement_code) PK.
database_design.denormalization: None needed.
database_design.transactions: Hint reveal is single update.

visualization_strategy: Hidden achievements show as `???` placeholder with question mark — mystery preserved. Hint offer is unobtrusive bottom-right corner — opt-in, never forced. Hint shown in amber/parchment color — feels like ancient scroll, magical. Rarity displayed only AFTER unlock ('Top 0.7%') — discovery dopamine. Color-blind: hidden state indicated by question mark icon, not just gray-out. Reduced motion: no animation on reveal.
balance_considerations: Watch hidden achievement discovery rate without hints (target 5-15% blind discovery) — too easy = not 'hidden' enough; too hard = nobody finds. Watch hint acceptance rate per tier (tier 1 ~30%, tier 2 ~50%, tier 3 ~70% — escalating help). Watch wiki search volume for achievements (community engagement signal).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Datamining achievement conditions — strings encrypted server-side, only revealed on unlock.
Hint scraping via API — hints only returned for achievements player has unlocked the tier for.
Spoiler trolling via shared screenshots — community moderation issue, not engineering.
observability: Log attempt counts, hint reveals, hidden_unlock events. Metrics: blind discovery rate, hint conversion (% who unlock after seeing hint), tier progression. Designer dashboard: 'too hard' (0 unlocks ever), 'too easy' (>50% unlock rate for hidden).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior shows hidden achievements as 'SECRET ACHIEVEMENT' with description blurred — kills mystery. Then: no hints, players quit looking after 10 attempts, achievement remains 0.01% unlock rate, community never discovers. Then: shown rarity makes blind unlockers feel ungratified ('I just got 0.3%!' but they didn't know it was 0.3% when they tried). Then: hints forced as popups — annoying, breaks immersion.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Returnal (datacube lore hints respect mystery), Fez (cryptic puzzle with community-driven discovery), Outer Wilds (entire game is hidden achievements). DONE BADLY: many Steam games title hidden achievements 'Hidden Achievement #1' which both spoils and demotivates. Lesson: mystery is value; preserve it without abandoning the player.

game_genre_context: Racing game (Forza Horizon, Mario Kart Tour, Asphalt) where leaderboards center on best lap times per track. Players grind a single track for hours shaving milliseconds. Time-based leaderboards have unique challenges (no skill rating, just raw times) but same anti-cheat needs.
title: Time-Based Leaderboard with Ghost Replays and Sector-Level Validation
design_intent: Build a per-track lap-time leaderboard that stores ghost replay data so players can race against #1's ghost, validates sector times (no impossible sector splits indicating cheating), preserves all-time bests across seasons, and shows percentile + nearby times. Forza Horizon's 'Rivals' mode set the gold standard. Trackmania's #1 ghosts are a content engine — every world record gets watched 100k times.
mathematical_foundation: Per track, store best time as float seconds. Sector validation: lap split into 4 sectors with min_sector_time per sector (based on theoretical optimum + 5% margin). Lap time = sum(sectors); validate sum matches reported lap time. Statistical baseline: track's distribution of lap times, anomaly threshold = 3σ below mean is suspect. Ghost data: 60Hz position+rotation samples, compressed via delta encoding. Storage: ~50KB per ghost lap.
psychology_notes: Time-based leaderboards are MERITOCRATIC — no luck, just skill. Players grind one track for weeks. Ghost playback lets you LEARN from the #1 — community education engine. Sector-level comparison ('you lost 0.3s in sector 2') is teaching. Personal bests displayed alongside global — 'beat your own best' is the core loop.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player completes lap, client sends sector times + total time + ghost data (compressed). Server validates: sectors sum to total, no sector below physical minimum, ghost data length matches lap time. If valid, compare to player's PB; if new PB, upload ghost. Compare to leaderboard; if top 1000, replace stored ghost.

2. **Math**: Sector minimum times calibrated from automated bot driving optimal racing line — anything faster than 95% of bot time is suspicious. Storage: keep top 1000 ghosts per track in S3 (50MB per track, fine).

3. **Edge cases**: Player using shortcut/glitch that legitimately exists — community-discovered glitches debated; either patch the glitch or accept it. Multiple top-10 times from same player — only PB counted on leaderboard (one entry per player). Track removed from rotation — leaderboards archived, ghosts preserved.

4. **Visual**: Top 100 with sector splits visible. 'View Ghost' button loads top player's run for replay. Personal PB chart over time. Heatmap of where YOU vs WR lose time.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All physics calculations server-replayable from input sequence. Suspicious ghost data triggers replay analysis — server reruns the inputs through physics, validates positions match.

6. **Rejected**: Trust client-reported time (cheat magnet), no ghost storage (community engagement lost), per-region only (small playerbase has dead leaderboards).

database_design.schema: Postgres lap_times: id, player_id, track_id, time_ms INT, sector_times INT[], ghost_url TEXT, recorded_at, validation_status. player_best: (player_id, track_id) → best lap_time_id. S3 bucket for ghost binary data.
database_design.indexes: lap_times: (track_id, time_ms ASC) for leaderboard. (player_id, track_id, time_ms ASC) for personal history. UNIQUE constraint (player_id, track_id) on player_best.
database_design.denormalization: Top 100 lap_times per track cached in Redis ZSET for fast leaderboard queries.
database_design.transactions: PB update: compare new time vs stored PB, update if better, atomic.

visualization_strategy: #1 gets gold gradient row. Player's own row highlighted cyan. Time displayed in canonical racing format (M:SS.ms). Ghost button explicit (eyeball ghost emoji = playful but clear). Sector comparison shows ms-precision deltas color-coded (red=slower, green=faster) — learning tool. Reduced motion: no transitions on data update. Color-blind: delta direction indicated by + / - prefix.
balance_considerations: Watch top-time progression — should improve weekly as players refine techniques. Watch ghost-watch rate per track (target >1 per top-100 entry). If new track sets WR in first hour that doesn't improve — either too easy or routing already discovered. A/B test sector-comparison UI vs total-only — players using comparison improve faster (engagement signal).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Submitting forged times — sector validation prevents fake totals.
Glitch exploitation — track-specific glitches debated by community; if banned, patched or filtered.
Replay manipulation — server replays input sequence through physics simulator (deterministic), validates result.
Ghost data stuffing — length validated against expected sample rate.
observability: Log every lap_submitted, validation_failed, flagged_for_review. Metrics: PB rate per track, ghost view rate, sector improvement over time. Dashboard: 'cheating wave detection' (spike in sub-WR submissions = new exploit).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior trusts client time, no sector check — cheaters set 0:00.01 lap times, leaderboard ruined, legit grinders quit. Then: no ghost storage — community can't learn from top times, content engine dead. Then: no sector validation — sector1 of 0.5s passes total-time check if other sectors compensate.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Trackmania (ghost-replay culture, sector splits), Forza Horizon Rivals, Mario Kart Tour. DONE BADLY: many 2010s racing games had time-based leaderboards with no anti-cheat, top 50 was all #0.001 times by week 2. Lesson: validate at the unit of measure — sectors, not totals.

game_genre_context: Player-driven trading economy MMO (EVE Online, Albion Online, Old School RuneScape). Players trade items with each other; the economy is a real market with supply/demand. Real-money trading (RMT) and bot farming are existential threats.
title: Player-Driven Auction House with Market Manipulation Detection
design_intent: Build an auction house where players list items at chosen prices, system matches buys/sells, takes a 5% fee (gold sink). Detect market manipulation (pump-and-dump, wash trading) and bot farming (suspicious price patterns, repetitive trades). RuneScape's Grand Exchange is the inspiration. EVE Online's market is the ultimate sandbox economy. WoW Diablo Immortal-style closed economies are simpler but less interesting; player-driven markets create emergent strategy.
mathematical_foundation: Order matching: bid/ask price-time priority (FIFO at price level). Market depth visualization: cumulative volume at each price band. Manipulation detection: rolling 24h price stddev; if a single seller's listings deviate >3σ from recent median, flag. Wash trade detection: two accounts trading same item back-and-forth at increasing prices = pump. Bot detection: gathering rate per hour > 99th percentile of player distribution. Fee = 5% of transaction value, paid by seller (standard market fee, removes gold from economy).
psychology_notes: Player-driven markets create emergent strategy — speculating, market-making, arbitrage. Players who don't play 'combat' play 'economy'. Price transparency (recent transactions visible) builds trust. The 5% fee psychologically frames the AH as 'real' market. Market manipulation if undetected destroys faith — once a few whales manipulate prices, normal players quit market.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player lists item with price. Order goes into matching engine. If matching buy exists, trade executes atomically (item to buyer, gold to seller minus fee). Else, sits in order book. Buyer can place limit buy that sits until seller listing meets price. Market data (recent trades, current bid/ask) public for transparency.

2. **Math**: FIFO matching at price level — fair and standard. 5% fee psychologically tolerable (real-world stock trading is lower; in-game with abundant gold, 5% is okay). Manipulation detection uses statistical baselines.

3. **Edge cases**: Both buyer and seller's prices match simultaneously — first listed wins (timestamp tiebreak). Item delisted while buy order pending — buy order returns to book. Server crash during match — transaction atomic, rolls back on failure.

4. **Visual**: Order book display (depth chart), recent trades scroll, price chart over time. List/buy modals with clear preview of fees. 'Your listings' section with status. 'Market trends' page for popular items.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All matching server-side. Trade history immutable. Manipulation detection via background job. Bot detection flags accounts gathering at impossible rates.

6. **Rejected**: NPC-only market (no emergent gameplay), no fees (gold inflation), unlimited bots (economy collapse).

database_design.schema: Postgres market_orders: id, player_id, item_code, side ENUM(buy,sell), price BIGINT, quantity INT, filled_quantity INT, status, listed_at, expires_at. market_trades: id, item_code, price, quantity, buyer_id, seller_id, fee_amount, executed_at. market_stats: item_code, last_price, volume_24h, median_price_7d.
database_design.indexes: market_orders: (item_code, side, price, listed_at) for matching. market_trades: (item_code, executed_at DESC) for charts.
database_design.denormalization: market_stats hourly rollup.
database_design.transactions: Order match is atomic: lock both orders, lock buyer gold + seller item, exchange, log trade.

visualization_strategy: Price chart with yellow line (gold = value) on dark background. Sell orders in rose (red = stop/danger to wallet), buy orders in emerald (green = inflow). Fee preview shown PROMINENTLY before commit — no hidden costs. Color-blind: order side indicated by label and chevron icon. Reduced motion: chart still animates because it's data, not decoration.
balance_considerations: Watch gold-in-circulation per item (faucet vs sink). Fee revenue = primary gold sink — track total fees collected as % of gold supply (target 2-5% removed per week). Watch wash-trade-flag rate — high = manipulation epidemic. Watch order book depth — thin = bots controlling supply.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Wash trading (self-trading to inflate volume) — detection job catches A↔B trades.
Pump-and-dump — manipulation detection on price stddev.
Bot farming items for sale — gather rate detection.
Front-running (canceling your buy when seeing other listings) — order book changes monitored.
Item dupe via match race — order matching atomic with row locks.
observability: Log all orders, trades, fees, manipulation flags. Metrics: order book depth per item, gold-removed-via-fees, manipulation flag rate, bot detection rate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior implements 'sell to NPC at fixed price' — no market dynamics, no emergent gameplay. Then: no fee = unlimited gold supply, inflation. Then: client-side bot detection = trivially bypassed. EVE Online's economy team has economists on staff for a reason.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: EVE Online (real economists), Old School RuneScape Grand Exchange. DONE BADLY: Diablo 3 Auction House — players bought best items, never grinded; loot became meaningless; expansion removed AH entirely. Lesson: a market must serve gameplay, not replace it.

game_genre_context: Mobile battle royale or .io game (Brawl Stars, Eggy Party, Stumble Guys) where match start latency matters as much as match quality. Mobile users have less patience — >30s queue and they swipe away.
title: Bot-Filled Quick-Match System with Skill-Hidden Bots and Latency-Optimized Region Selection
design_intent: Build a quick-match system that starts matches within 5 seconds by filling empty slots with skill-calibrated bots when real players aren't immediately available. Bots are indistinguishable from new players (no '_BOT' name suffix), have realistic behavior patterns. As real players join the queue, bots are swapped out mid-lobby. Brawl Stars uses this; Fall Guys does NOT (and queues are sometimes 60s, but their player base is huge enough). Mobile games depend on this — without it, casual players churn.
mathematical_foundation: Queue wait threshold T: if no match found in T seconds, fill remaining slots with bots. T scales with player skill: novice T=3s, expert T=8s (experts more tolerant of waiting for real match). Bot skill calibrated to player's MMR ± 10%, slight bias toward easier (better player experience).
