Match quality with bots: target win rate 55% for player (slightly above 50%) — bots tuned to lose slightly more often. Bot-swap rate: while in lobby, every 5s check queue, swap bot for real player.
psychology_notes: 5-second match start beats 60-second wait — most casual players don't have 60-second attention spans on phone. Bots that PRETEND to be humans (real-looking usernames, realistic latency on actions) extend the illusion. The 55% win rate target keeps casual players happy without making the game feel rigged. As players progress, T threshold extends and bot quality improves; eventually high-skill matches are real-only.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player taps 'Quick Match'. Server: scan queue for matching skill within tolerance. If full lobby found in T seconds, start match. Else fill with bots from bot pool (each bot has its own profile with realistic name, avatar, MMR). Lobby phase: every 5s, scan queue, swap bots for real players.

2. **Math**: Bot MMR calibrated to lobby's median MMR ± 10%. Slight downward bias (55% win rate target). Bot pool sized to 10x concurrent players for unique-name pool. Real-player priority over bots during swap.

3. **Edge cases**: All-bot game starts, no real players join — fine, player has fun, no one notices. Bot 'wins' but it's a bot — bot-victories don't count toward leaderboards (only player-vs-player results). Bot mid-match swap: only at lobby phase, never mid-match (state continuity).

4. **Visual**: No visual indication of bots. Lobby shows all 'players' with avatars and names. Loading screen identical regardless of bot count. Post-match results may show 'AFK detected' or similar generic for bot 'losses' if necessary.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Bots aren't winnable cheats. Real human players can't intentionally play bots (matched by skill). Bot detection algorithms try to identify human-controlled accounts mimicking bots (rare scenario).

6. **Rejected**: Long real-only queues (mobile churn), obvious bot labels (kills illusion), all-bot games disguised as PvP for ranked (cheats player out of real competition).

database_design.schema: Bots are virtual: stored as bot_profiles: id, name, avatar_url, mmr, behavior_pattern. Match record stores both real player_ids and bot_ids (with is_bot flag) for analytics. Real-MMR updates only for real-player matchups.
database_design.indexes: bot_profiles: (mmr) for skill-band lookups.
database_design.denormalization: None needed.
database_design.transactions: Match initialization atomic.

visualization_strategy: No bot indication. Lobby looks the same regardless. Searching animation is brief — under 5s total. Match-start moment same drama whether bot-filled or real. Reduced motion: no spinner, just text.
balance_considerations: Watch player win rate (target 50-55% globally, slightly higher for novices). Watch real-vs-bot match ratio per skill bucket — at high skill, should be 100% real. Bot detection by community (Reddit posts identifying bot behaviors) — if visible, tune bot AI more realistic. Watch retention by 'first 5 matches' results — losing all 5 = high churn; bot-assisted wins prevent this.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Forced bot matches to farm easy wins — bot wins don't count toward ranked/leaderboards.
Bot detection by player to throw matches — bot identity hidden; no API endpoint reveals isBot.
Smurfs farming new player lobbies (queue at low MMR) — separate matchmaking pool for verified-new accounts.
observability: Log match composition (bot count, real count), session retention, win rates by experience. Metrics: avg queue time, bot-fill rate, mid-match swap success rate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior makes queues real-only — mobile players uninstall after one 60s wait. Then: bots clearly labeled — illusion broken, players feel cheated. Then: bots too strong — new players quit. Bots too weak — players don't improve, can't transition to PvP.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Brawl Stars (seamless bots), Among Us early access. DONE BADLY: many mobile games where 'PvP' is 90% bots even at high skill — when discovered, refund storms. Lesson: bots are a courtesy, not deception.

game_genre_context: Web-based collectible card game character preview (Hearthstone-like). Characters appear in 3D portraits with idle animations, signature moves, and shader-driven effects. The character roster screen IS the marketing showcase.
title: Character Showcase with Idle Animations, Signature Pose Reactions, and Shader-Based Status Effects
design_intent: Build a 3D character viewer with idle breathing animations, mouse-hover reactions (turn head toward cursor), signature pose triggered by click, and shader-based 'on fire' / 'frozen' / 'poisoned' overlays. Lots of mobile/web card games skip this and lose 'collection' identity. Marvel Snap and Hearthstone Battlegrounds use animated portraits to make heroes feel ALIVE — players bond with characters that breathe.
mathematical_foundation: Idle animation: sine-wave breathing scale (1 + 0.02 * sin(t * 1.2)) on chest bone. Head-look-at: lerp between rest rotation and look-at-mouse quaternion, clamped to ±25° per axis. Status shader: dissolve effect uses noise texture sampled with threshold; 'on fire' is animated normal-map distortion + emissive heat-glow. Performance budget: 3 characters visible × 5000 triangles each + 60fps on mid-tier mobile.
psychology_notes: Idle breathing = 'alive'. Head turning to cursor = 'sees you'. These tiny touches build attachment. Click-to-trigger-pose = 'I command you'. Status effects shaders communicate game state visually rather than text — fire effect overlay = 'burning' without reading combat log.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Component mounts, loads GLTF model + animation clips. useFrame ticks breathing animation. Mouse pos updates head bone target. Click triggers signature animation clip. Status effects applied via shader uniform updates.

2. **Math**: Smooth lerping prevents jitter; quaternion slerp for rotation avoids gimbal lock.

3. **Edge cases**: Multi-character grid — instanced rendering for performance. Low-end device — fallback to static portrait. Status effects stack — combine via shader passes.

4. **Visual**: Subtle breathing always. Hover triggers head-turn + slight smile (eye-track). Click plays signature animation with particle burst. Status overlays distinct (fire = orange flicker, frozen = blue crystal layer).

5. **Anti-exploit**: No exploit vectors (cosmetic only).

6. **Rejected**: Static portraits (loses character identity), pre-rendered video (no interactivity), full game-quality models (perf cost).

database_design.schema: characters: id, name, model_url, animations JSONB ({ idle, signature, attack, dies }), portrait_2d_fallback_url.
database_design.indexes: characters: (id) PK.
database_design.denormalization: None.
database_design.transactions: None needed.

backend_solution: // Minimal — mostly CDN-served assets. Character metadata via simple GET.\nexport async function getCharacterShowcase(characterId: string) { return db.one('SELECT * FROM characters WHERE id=$1', [characterId]); }
visualization_strategy: Subtle breathing always — character feels alive. Head-tracking creates 'they see me' moment. Click triggers signature with particles. Status effects shader-driven, not particle overlay (cleaner, performant). Reduced motion: static portrait, no breathing/tracking. Color-blind: status effects use distinct icons in lower corner as well as color.
balance_considerations: Watch session length on character roster page — well-tuned characters keep players browsing for 'I want to use that one'. Track click rate per character (signature trigger interest). FPS on mid-tier mobile — must stay 60fps with 3 characters showing.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Asset extraction — models served behind auth proxy with signed URLs.
Showcasing characters not owned (fake screenshots) — server validates ownership before showcase render.
observability: Log character_view, signature_triggered, time_on_roster. Metrics: per-character engagement, FPS distribution.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior uses static PNG portraits — players don't bond with characters, collection feels like spreadsheet. Then: shaders cause FPS drops on mid-tier devices — game feels janky.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Hearthstone Battlegrounds hero portraits, Marvel Snap card art. DONE BADLY: many indie card games with static art that ages poorly. Lesson: animation = identity.

game_genre_context: Mobile social game (Pokémon Go, Niantic-style) with friend lists, gifting, and limited communication to prevent harassment. The friend system enables coordinated play without text chat (a deliberate Niantic safety choice).
title: Friend System with Gift Cap, Lucky Friend Bonuses, and Mutual-Only Visibility
design_intent: Build a friend list where players send daily gifts (free items from gameplay locations), with Pokémon Go's 'Lucky Friend' bonus system (5% chance per gift that both friends get bonus). Players are only visible to mutual friends — no random-strangers DMs to prevent harassment. Friend tiers (Good→Great→Ultra→Best) unlocked by daily interactions, with rewards at each tier. This is Niantic's secret weapon — friend-driven retention with minimal moderation overhead.
mathematical_foundation: Friend tier progression by daily interactions (1 per day max per friend pair): Good=0, Great=7, Ultra=30, Best=90. Tier-up reward = base_xp * pow(2, tier_level). Lucky Friend probability per gift = 0.05; if both already 'lucky' on cooldown (24h), suppressed. Daily gift cap per player = 20 sends, 100 opens. Mutual-only visibility: friend appears in your list iff both have accepted.
psychology_notes: Friend tier progression turns acquaintances into 'best friends' — achievement loop within social. Lucky Friend RNG creates 'I love opening gifts!' anticipation. Gift caps prevent farming exploitation. No public profiles for non-friends prevents stalking/harassment — Niantic learned this from Pokémon Go community feedback.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player sends gift → server creates pending_gift to recipient → recipient opens at convenience → both receive items + interaction increment. Daily interaction max 1 per pair. Tier check on increment.

2. **Math**: Tier thresholds tuned to '3 months to Best Friend' which is the bonding window.

3. **Edge cases**: Friend deletes account — friendship dissolves cleanly, no orphan data. Gift sent but recipient hits cap — gift queued for next day. Blocked user — friend status auto-broken.

4. **Visual**: Friend list with tier badges. Gift inbox separate from notifications (curated experience). Tier-up celebration with both friends' avatars.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Gift caps prevent farming. Mutual visibility prevents griefing. Block functionality severs all connection.

6. **Rejected**: Unlimited gifts (RMT bait), public profiles (harassment vector), no tier system (no friendship goal).

database_design.schema: friendships: (player_a, player_b, tier, interaction_count, last_interaction_at, became_friends_at). pending_gifts: id, sender_id, recipient_id, items JSONB, created_at, opened_at. daily_gift_counts: player_id, date, sent_count, opened_count.
database_design.indexes: friendships: (player_a), (player_b) — composite PK on (LEAST(a,b), GREATEST(a,b)). pending_gifts: (recipient_id, opened_at) for inbox queries.
database_design.denormalization: Friend count cached on player.
database_design.transactions: Tier-up + reward grant atomic.

visualization_strategy: Tier badges use universal color hierarchy (slate→blue→purple→gold). Gift open shows lucky banner in gold if triggered. Tier-up celebration uses both friends' names — relational moment. Reduced motion: no animations. Color-blind: tier indicated by badge text + icon (star count).
balance_considerations: Watch friendship tier-up rate — target 30% reach Best Friend within 6 months. Watch gift-send rate — should max out at cap for active players. Lucky Friend feedback — track whether lucky moments drive return visits.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Gift farming via alt accounts — daily caps prevent.
Stalking via public profile — mutual-only visibility.
Harassment via gift spam — gift cap, block functionality severs all interactions.
observability: Log gifts_sent, gifts_opened, tier_ups, lucky_events. Metrics: friend count distribution, tier progression curves, retention by friend count.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior implements public friend search — leads to stalking incidents, PR disaster. Then: no gift caps, alt accounts farm rare items via gifting. Then: no tier system — friends feel transactional, no bonding loop.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Pokémon Go friend system (model). DONE BADLY: WoW classic-era friend list with no mutual-confirm — stalking issues for high-profile players. Lesson: friends must be mutual; visibility must be earned.

game_genre_context: Web-based browser puzzle game with leaderboards (Wordle competitive, casual.gg style competitions). Browser games face uniquely difficult anti-cheat — no kernel access, easy DevTools. The cheating problem is solved through obfuscation + statistical detection + replay verification, not hardware.
title: Browser-Game Replay-Verification Anti-Cheat with Solve-Time Statistical Outlier Detection
design_intent: Build an anti-cheat for a browser puzzle game where the server requires a FULL replay (every keystroke, every click, with timestamps) to validate a submission. Server re-runs the replay through the puzzle simulator deterministically — output must match. Additionally, statistical detection on solve times catches bots (impossibly fast) and macros (impossibly consistent timing). This is how Wordle's competitive cousins protect leaderboards.
mathematical_foundation: Replay event log: every keystroke with t_offset_ms from puzzle start. Server replays via same simulator code that runs in browser — if final state differs from claimed, reject. Statistical detection: per-user solve time distribution; flag if user's times are <99.9% percentile baseline OR have low variance (perfectly consistent = macro). Variance threshold: if user's std/mean < 0.05 over 20 solves, flag.
psychology_notes: Browser-game cheating is rampant because tooling is trivial. Catching cheats invisibly preserves leaderboard trust without revealing detection methods to cheaters. Replay verification means even DevTools modification of game state is caught because the input log doesn't match the result.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player solves puzzle. Client builds full event log (timestamp, action). On submit, replay POSTed to server. Server runs deterministic simulator over events, verifies final state. Records timing stats. Background job analyzes statistical patterns.

2. **Math**: Coefficient of variation (std/mean) of solve times for legit humans is 0.15-0.3. Macros produce 0.02-0.05 — extreme consistency. Threshold 0.05 catches macros without false-positiving fast-but-human players.

3. **Edge cases**: Replay too long (>10MB) — reject as suspicious. Replay missing events — reject. Player on slow device with input lag — timing offsets within tolerance.

4. **Visual**: No anti-cheat UI to players. Banned players get vague 'submission could not be verified' — no detection method revealed.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All puzzle logic server-replayable. Client randomization seeded from server.

6. **Rejected**: Trust client-reported solve (cheat playground), tedious manual review of every submission (doesn't scale).

database_design.schema: puzzle_submissions: id, player_id, puzzle_id, claimed_time_ms, replay JSONB, server_verified BOOLEAN, anomaly_flags JSONB, submitted_at. player_solve_stats: player_id, puzzle_set, mean_time NUMERIC, std_dev NUMERIC, solve_count INT.
database_design.indexes: puzzle_submissions: (player_id, puzzle_id) UNIQUE, (puzzle_id, claimed_time_ms ASC) for leaderboard.
database_design.denormalization: player_solve_stats rolled up after each verified submit.
database_design.transactions: Verification + leaderboard update atomic.

visualization_strategy: No anti-cheat visualization — players don't see the system. Submitting puzzle just gives 'verified' confirmation or vague 'could not verify' message. Verified submissions show on leaderboard normally. Reduced motion: standard. Color-blind: pass — no visual encoding involved.
balance_considerations: Watch verified-rate (target >99% of legitimate plays verified). Watch false-positive complaints (target <0.01%). Watch macro-detection rate per day — rising = macro-tool went viral, needs response.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Submitting fabricated solve times — replay must produce the time when re-simulated.
Macros — solve-time variance detection.
Bots — sub-human solve speed detection.
Replay tampering — replay events validated for monotonic time, plausible action delays.
observability: Log every submission, verification result, flag type. Metrics: verification rate, anomaly flag rate, macro detection rate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior trusts client-reported time — leaderboard fills with 0.001ms 'solves'. Then: replay-only, no statistical check — macros pass replay verification (they ARE replaying real solves, just superhumanly consistent). Then: no rate-limit — bots farm leaderboards in seconds.
real_world_inspiration: DONE WELL: Tetr.io (replay-based anti-cheat for competitive Tetris), Speedrun.com submission verification process. DONE BADLY: many flash-game era leaderboards had no validation, top scores all fake within days, no one took them seriously. Lesson: leaderboard integrity = product.
